messages.content: You must return JSON only. ALL fields in the schema must be present, even if null or empty.

Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_raw: {"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","persona":{"age":84,"gender":"woman","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"not applicable","city_of_living":"Milan","employment_status":"retired","education_level":"upper secondary","parents_education":{"parent_1":"PhD","parent_2":"no formal education"},"current_marital_status":"married","children":4,"migration_status":"immigrant","psychological_feature":"anxious","religious_beliefs":"Christianity","time_onsocialmedia":"You have no social media","hobbies":["cinema","classical music"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":54,"level":"moderate"},"conscientiousness":{"score":72,"level":"high"},"extraversion":{"score":68,"level":"high"},"agreeableness":{"score":13,"level":"low"},"neuroticism":{"score":45,"level":"moderate"}}},"opinion":"In my time, we didn't have these social media things. We had newspapers, radio, and television. If something was false, it would take time to spread. Now, with these new technologies, false information can spread like wildfire. I worry about my grandchildren, who are always on their phones. They see something on the internet, and they believe it's true. I remember when one of them showed me a story about a famous actor who had passed away. I thought it was true, but later I found out it was a lie. It's scary how easily these lies can spread. I think the best way to deal with fake content is to be careful about what you believe. If something seems too good or too bad to be true, it probably is. I also think it's important to talk to your family about this. Teach them to question what they see and to look for reliable sources. I don't use social media, but I know it's important to be aware of these things. I also think that the people who run these social media sites should do more to stop the spread of false information. They have a responsibility to their users. I remember when I was young, we had a saying: 'Don't believe everything you hear.' That's still good advice today. We need to be critical thinkers and not just accept everything we see on the internet. It's a big world out there, and not everything is as it seems. We need to be careful and protect ourselves and our families from these lies.","reasoning_summary":"The persona is an 84-year-old woman who is retired and does not use social media. She is concerned about the spread of fake content on social media and the impact it has on her grandchildren. She also thinks that social media companies should do more to stop the spread of false information.","tone":"concerned, protective, traditional","citations":["Personal experience and observations"],"safety_notes":"none","used_persona_fields":["age","gender","occupation","city_of_living","employment_status","education_level","parents_education","current_marital_status","children","migration_status","psychological_feature","religious_beliefs","time_onsocialmedia","hobbies","ocean"]}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: In my time, we didn't have these social media things. We had newspapers, radio, and television. If something was false, it would take time to spread. Now, with these new technologies, false information can spread like wildfire. I worry about my grandchildren, who are always on their phones. They see something on the internet, and they believe it's true. I remember when one of them showed me a story about a famous actor who had passed away. I thought it was true, but later I found out it was a lie. It's scary how easily these lies can spread. I think the best way to deal with fake content is to be careful about what you believe. If something seems too good or too bad to be true, it probably is. I also think it's important to talk to your family about this. Teach them to question what they see and to look for reliable sources. I don't use social media, but I know it's important to be aware of these things. I also think that the people who run these social media sites should do more to stop the spread of false information. They have a responsibility to their users. I remember when I was young, we had a saying: 'Don't believe everything you hear.' That's still good advice today. We need to be critical thinkers and not just accept everything we see on the internet. It's a big world out there, and not everything is as it seems. We need to be careful and protect ourselves and our families from these lies.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is an 84-year-old woman who is retired and does not use social media. She is anxious and has a moderate level of openness and neuroticism. She is highly conscientious and extraverted but low in agreeableness. She is a Christian and has four children. She is concerned about the spread of fake content on social media and the impact it has on her grandchildren. She believes that the best way to deal with fake content is to be careful about what you believe and to teach your family to be critical thinkers. She also thinks that social media companies should do more to stop the spread of false information.
